coredata.dc:title: Thermophoretic sampling of large PAH (C ≥ 22–24) formed in flames
coredata.dc:creator: Russo, Carmela
Apicella, Barbara
Tregrossi, Antonio
Oliano, Maria Maddalena
Ciajolo, Anna
coredata.dc:description: The study of the transition from molecular to particle-like species involved in soot inception needs experimental strategies aiming to measure high molecular mass structures that could be important also for their effect on environment and human health. In this work, we have investigated the carbon particulate selectivity involved in the thermophoretic deposition used for the first time as a direct and fast sampling of high molecular weight aromatic species formed at the inception stage in an ethylene heavily sooting flame. Gravimetric analysis along with mass spectrometry, UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy showed that the dichloromethane-extract of carbon particulate matter thermophoretically deposited on the substrate was much less than that sampled by a conventional water-cooled probe as it is composed only of high molecular weight condensed phases (C ≥ 22–24). Thermophoretic deposition is demonstrated to pre-separate, already at the sampling step, soot precursors of high molecular weight allowing their further analysis without the interference of the more abundant light polycyclic aromatic compounds (C < 22–24). These high molecular weight aromatic species presented interesting optical properties in terms of size and fluorescence emission peaked in the blue-region that could be of interest for carbon dots application. Size exclusion chromatography of particulate coupled with UV–Visible spectroscopy showed the presence of some aggregates already at the beginning of soot formation to be taken into account as ruling the optical properties of the whole carbon particulate matter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Soot
Large PAH
Soot precursors
Thermophoretic sampling

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: 1st International Conference on Smart Energy Carriers – SMARTCATs COST Action

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mass absorption coefficients (MAC) (left part) and normalized Raman spectra (right part) of substrate (black line) and probe soot (grey line) in the inception region (6 mm HAB) of the ethylene flame.

SEC chromatogram of substrate soot collected at 6 mm HAB (left part), and normalized UV–Vis spectra of the main MW-segregated fractions measured by SEC (right part). SEC chromatogram of soot fraction <20 nm is also reported.
Mass absorption coefficients (MAC) of substrate (black line) and probe (grey line) DCM-extract collected at 6 mm HAB.

Fluorescence emission excited at 350 nm (left part) and synchronous fluorescence spectra (Δλ = 10 nm) (right part) of substrate (black line) and probe (grey line) DCM-extract at 6 mm HAB. A synchronous fluorescence spectrum of a mixture of two- to seven-ring PAH standards [51] is also reported for comparison.
Atmospheric Pressure Photoionization–Mass Spectrometry (APPI-MS) spectra of (a) toluene, (b) probe DCM-extract sampled by at 6 mm HAB and (c) substrate DCM-extract sampled at 6 mm HAB.
Weight percentages of DCM-extract fraction of the total carbon particulate formed along the flame and collected by means of substrate deposition (black bar) and sampling probe (grey bar).

Optical band gap of substrate soot deposited at 5, 6, 7 mm HAB evaluated on the spectrum of the bulk particulate matter suspended in NMP and on the on-line UV–Vis spectra measured on the apex of the main SEC peaks.

head.ce:title: Thermophoretic sampling of large PAH (C ≥ 22–24) formed in flames

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istituto di Ricerche sulla Combustione – C.N.R., Napoli, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Istituto di Ricerche sulla Combustione – C.N.R.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istituto di Ricerche sulla Combustione – C.N.R., Napoli, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study of the transition from molecular to particle-like species involved in soot inception needs experimental strategies aiming to measure high molecular mass structures that could be important also for their effect on environment and human health. In this work, we have investigated the carbon particulate selectivity involved in the thermophoretic deposition used for the first time as a direct and fast sampling of high molecular weight aromatic species formed at the inception stage in an ethylene heavily sooting flame. Gravimetric analysis along with mass spectrometry, UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy showed that the dichloromethane-extract of carbon particulate matter thermophoretically deposited on the substrate was much less than that sampled by a conventional water-cooled probe as it is composed only of high molecular weight condensed phases (C ≥ 22–24). Thermophoretic deposition is demonstrated to pre-separate, already at the sampling step, soot precursors of high molecular weight allowing their further analysis without the interference of the more abundant light polycyclic aromatic compounds (C < 22–24). These high molecular weight aromatic species presented interesting optical properties in terms of size and fluorescence emission peaked in the blue-region that could be of interest for carbon dots application. Size exclusion chromatography of particulate coupled with UV–Visible spectroscopy showed the presence of some aggregates already at the beginning of soot formation to be taken into account as ruling the optical properties of the whole carbon particulate matter.

ce:section.ce:para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) formation and particle inception followed by coagulation, surface growth, aggregation and oxidation, concur to soot formation and emission. Soot formation has been studied extensively in the last decades and periodically updated in review papers presented at round tables [1–3]. Specifically, the current view of soot formation starts with particle nucleation, followed by mass and size growth through phenomena often concurrent as coalescence, coagulation, surface reactions, and aggregation into fractal structures. One of the main unresolved part of soot formation picture is the transition from the gas-phase to the condensed phase with the identification of precursor species and physical/chemical processes preferentially involved in this step.

The study of the transformation from molecule-like to particle- and nanoparticle-like species needs experimental strategies aiming to measure high molecular mass structures. From the experimental point of view, the problem has been tackled time-by-time developing and implementing advanced techniques, however it is not easy to separate and measure these species as in most cases both soot particles and soot precursors coexist for long reaction times [3,4]. Moreover, it has been suggested [5], and recently experimentally observed both in diffusion and laminar premixed flames [6–8], that soot particle aggregation is a phenomenon not well separated in time from particle nucleation, hence hindering the easy detection of the first particle inception. The high resolution and negligible intrusiveness of the optical diagnostics as light extinction and laser induced incandescence [4,9–16] can overcome this problem and can be applied in the soot inception region for detecting soot particles as well as soot precursors (defined as species with molecular weight (MW) > 300 u [4]) in gas or in condensed phase [4,9].

The very small nanometric size of nascent soot particles has been found by sampling coupled to a scanning mobility particle sizer (SMPS) and differential mobility analysis (DMA) [17–21]. Imaging of the first particles at a nanoscale level has been achieved by batch sampling followed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) [e.g. 22–25] or helium‐ion microscopy (HIM) [26]. Additional information can be obtained by ex-situ chemical/spectroscopic analysis of nascent soot. This analysis requires long sampling times for recovering measurable amounts of nascent soot so that few works on chemical and structural analysis have been performed hinting to relatively low percentages of sp2 carbon and small extension of the aromatic layer, associated to high H/C ratios and low visible-absorption coefficients of nascent soot [23–25,27].

Organic species accompanying soot formation and mostly constituted of PAH have been analyzed by both in-situ [4,9,28–29] and ex-situ advanced methods like mass spectrometry [30,31] and size exclusion chromatography (SEC) [32,33] individuating low and medium molecular weight PAH. Interestingly enough a bimodal distribution of PAH peaked in the low and high molecular weight, below and above 400 u, respectively, has been found by mass spectrometry [34] and SEC techniques [35] of flame products. In particular, the characterization of the high molecular weight fraction has been found difficult hindering the attribution of these species to dimers or large PAH which could help the knowledge on soot inception mechanism.

The physical and chemical state of PAH species which could be present in form of molecules, aggregates or clusters is important for their effective sampling, detection and identification and, more importantly, for distinguishing which PAH species are merely side products of fuel-rich combustion reactions or have a specific role in soot inception. Very few works have been devoted to establish the partitioning of PAH in gas-and condensed matter in the flame environment [36,37]. It has been found that two-ring, three-four ring PAH are abundantly present in gas phase and so classified as volatile semi-volatile PAH, while PAH comprising more than four rings and classified as non-volatile PAH are primarily trapped in soot. Beside to be less prone to conventional analysis for their low volatility and thermal degradability, the high molecular weight fraction of organic carbon is particularly difficult to be characterized also because of the admixture with light compounds overcoming and hindering their separation and qualification. Several methods as solvent extraction or size exclusion chromatography can be used to separate the organic carbon in its components, but these methods are very time-consuming and not completely successful for a complete separation of light PAH from the tarry matrix [32,33,38–41]. The present work reports the details of a thermophoretic deposition method implemented on a heavily sooting laminar premixed ethylene flame (equivalence ratio = 3) [42], as selective sampling of nascent soot and soot precursors deprived of the low-MW PAH species.

Specifically, to demonstrate the effectiveness of thermophoretic sampling in sooting premixed flames the characteristics of soot and soot precursors sampled by thermophoresis on a substrate (hereafter named as substrate samples) and by a well-assessed sampling mode, i.e. a water-cooled probe (hereafter named probe samples) have been deeply analyzed by UV–Visible, fluorescence and Raman spectroscopic tools along with size exclusion chromatography and mass spectrometry.

Carbon particulate matter was sampled in an ethylene-oxygen premixed laminar atmospheric flame (C/O = 1, cold gas velocity = 4 cm/s) burning on a water-cooled sintered bronze McKenna burner (d = 60 mm) (Holthuis & Associates). More details on the flame structure are reported in [42]. A glass plate (75 × 25 × 1 mm) was used to thermophoretically collect carbon particulate matter deposited at 5, 6, 7 mm of height above the burner (HAB), i.e. in the region of soot inception [42]. The set-up for the deposition into the flame was constituted of a glass plate horizontally inserted into the flame and rotating by a gear motor with a rotation speed of 1.4 gear/s. The insertion/deposition time of the plate was regulated at 60 ms per lap to avoid the plate heating. The total deposition time for each sample was set to 25 s. Preliminary tests were performed in order to verify that the exposure time does not affect soot properties. In particular, Raman and UV–Visible spectroscopy on samples collected with different deposition times (from 100 ms to 50 s) have shown that there were no differences of the sampled particles. The thermophoretic sampling was faster in comparison to a water-cooled probe that required longer sampling times for recovering measurable amounts of soot, especially in the first phase of soot formation. It has been calculated that at 6 mm HAB 0.1 mg/s and 0.001 mg/s of soot were recovered by thermophoretic and probe sampling, respectively.

The carbon particulate matter deposited on the substrate was scratched from the plate and extracted with dichloromethane (DCM) to separate organic carbon from the solid carbon (soot). Soot was then dispersed in N-methyl-pyrrolidinone (NMP) and filtered on Anodisc filters for separating soot particles with diameter > 20 nm from NMP solubles (<20 nm). The amount of soot deposited on the Anodisc filter was dried and gravimetrically measured.

Raman spectra of soot powder obtained after DCM extraction of carbon particulate matter were measured by means of a Horiba XploRA Raman microscope system (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of soot and DCM-extract were measured in a 1-cm quartz cell by using a HP 8453 spectrophotometer. The mass absorption coefficient of soot and DCM-extract was determined on known concentration (concentration = 10 mg/l) of sample suspended in NMP. Fluorescence spectra of DCM-extract were acquired on a PerkinElmer LS-50 spectrofluorimeter using a xenon discharge lamp as excitation light source and a gated photomultiplier with modified S5 response for operation to about 650 nm as detection device. Monochromators were of the Monk-Gillieson type and covered the following ranges: excitation of 200–800 nm and emission of 200–900 nm. The wavelength accuracy was ± 1.0 nm, and the wavelength reproducibility was ± 0.5 nm. Fluorescence measurements were performed on samples very diluted in NMP to avoid concentration quenching and/or other phenomena that could distort the spectral shape affecting the fluorescence intensity. Synchronous fluorescence emission spectra were measured by applying a simultaneous scanning of the excitation and emission wavelengths, keeping constant the difference between the wavelengths (Δλ = 10 nm).

The APPI mass spectra of DCM-extracts were obtained on an Agilent 1100 Series MSD Trap (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The DCM-extract samples, dried and dissolved in toluene, were directly injected into the APPI source by using a syringe pump (5 ml/min). A PhotoMate orthogonal APPI spray source (Syagen Technology, Tustin, CA, USA) was installed on the mass spectrometer. The APPI source was based on a radio frequency (RF) discharge of a gas mixture consisting primarily of krypton and operated on the atomic emission lines at 10.0 and 10.6 eV. More details on the mass spectrometric system are reported in [31].

Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) of soot suspensions in NMP was carried out on a HPLC system HP1050 series by elution with NMP on a Jordi Gel DVB Solid Bead column 300 × 7.8 mm for the molecular weight (MW) determination in the 1E5-1E11u range. The online detection of species eluted from the SEC column used a HP1050 UV–Visible diode array detector that measured the absorbance signal at a fixed absorption wavelength (350 nm).

Soot samples collected by thermophoretic deposition on a glass plate and soot sampled by a water-cooled probe, hereafter named as substrate and probe soot respectively, have been first analyzed by UV–Vis and Raman spectroscopy, signatures of aromaticity and carbon network. The UV–Vis and Raman spectra of both soot samples are compared in Fig. 1 for the first flame position (6 mm HAB) in the soot inception region where soot sampled by probe was enough to perform spectroscopic analysis.

The UV–Vis spectra of soot, formerly subjected to DCM extraction and then suspended in NMP, are reported in the left panel of Fig. 1 in form of mass absorption coefficient (MAC) against the wavelength. The absorption decrease from the UV toward the visible, typical of soot, is noticeable for both substrate and probe soot, and only a slightly higher MAC can be observed for the substrate soot. It is also noteworthy that the MAC in the visible (550 nm) is very low (around 2 m2/g), next to the lower limit of the MAC range (2–7 m2/g) typically measured for soot from different sources (see Table 6 in [43]). The low absorption reflects a relatively low sp2 aromatic content evaluated for nascent/young soot in previous work [8,24].

Also the Raman spectra of the substrate and probe soot are rather similar displaying the two typical peaks of carbon materials, near 1600 cm−1 (G or “graphite” peak) and 1350 cm−1 (D or “defect” peak), in the first order spectral Raman region, between 1000 and 1800 cm−1 [44]. The spectra present other interesting features as the D and G peak broadness and a relevant photoluminescence underlying the Raman scattering signal that is a signature of the high hydrogen content of soot [45,46]. Both inferences hint to the highly disordered structure of soot. The high disorder degree is also testified by other minor modulations in the Raman spectra, induced by defects outside and inside the crystal lattice. Overall, UV–Vis and Raman spectra of substrate and probe soot match very satisfactorily underlying their similarity in terms of nanostructure and composition, and giving evidence that there is no effect of the different collection procedure (thermophoretic deposition and probe sampling) on the carbon network structure of soot particles/aggregates.

Also the separation of soot in size/MW-segregated fractions, carried out by SEC analysis, showed the similarity of substrate and probe soot so that in Fig. 2 only the chromatogram obtained by SEC analysis of substrate soot is reported. For “reading” the SEC profile it is worth to underline that the SEC elution proceeds in order of decreasing size as the elution time increases [47]. The typical multimodality of soot particle size distribution can be clearly observed in Fig. 2. By calibration with differently-sized polystyrene and carbon standards, the first peak (peak 1) can be ascribed to particle aggregates (about 100 nm), peak 2 is due to single particles (10–20 nm) whereas residual organic carbon not removed by DCM-extraction is responsible for peak 3 [47].

The presence of the aggregate peak (peak 1) even for the soot sample collected in the soot inception region, testifies the occurrence of aggregation to some extent since the beginning of soot formation, at least in the heavily-sooting condition of the flame investigated. It is also noteworthy that the peak 1 is absent in the SEC profile of soot filtered on 20 nm filters also reported in Fig. 2 confirming the attribution of peak 1 to soot size > 20 nm. Soot having size > 20 nm deposited on the Anodisc filter has been evaluated by means of gravimetric measurements. It has been found that the weight percentage of soot > 20 nm of the bulk soot increases along the flame from 15 wt% at 5 mm to 35% at 6 mm, up to 64% at 7 mm HAB.

The energy band gap was evaluated by using the Tauc relation: (A × E)0.5 = B × (E − Eg) [48], where A is the absorbance, E is the energy of the incident photon, B is a constant and Eg is the optical band gap evaluated on the visible tail of the Tauc spectra by extrapolation to zero absorption. The optical band gap is related in a complex manner to soot nanostructure [8,39,40,49] and morphology since secondary effects on electronic delocalization may also occur due to interparticle/intraparticle tunneling effect [8]. The optical band gap values, Eg, reported in Table 1 have been evaluated on the on-line spectra collected on the apex of the SEC peaks representative of the main MW-segregated fractions of soot thermophoretically sampled at 5, 6, 7 mm HAB. The band gap values evaluated on the UV–Vis spectrum of the bulk soot suspension are also reported in Table 1.

It can be seen that the band gap evaluated on the bulk particulate matter is closer to that evaluated on the on-line spectrum measured for the aggregate peak (peak 1), indicating that the optical/electronic properties of soot are nearer to that of the soot aggregates (peak 1) in spite of their lower contribution to size distribution (Fig. 2). Only in the case of 5 mm HAB the band gap of bulk soot exhibits an intermediate value between those evaluated for the peaks 1 and 2 possibly due to the fact that aggregates represent just the 15 wt% of the bulk soot samples. It can be inferred that the low optical band gap of soot is mainly determined by soot aggregates (even few) and this inference has to be taken into account when using the optical band gap to follow soot formation/transformations.
The MAC in the whole UV-Vis range of the substrate and probe DCM-extract collected at 6 mm HAB are compared in Fig. 3 .

The MAC values of the DCM-extract sampled by probe are very high in the UV region, while present much lower values in the visible region, i.e. the DCM-extract presents a higher wavelength dependence in comparison to soot. This feature along with the appearance of some fine spectral structure is typical of polycyclic aromatic structures. Fig. 4 reports fluorescence emission spectra excited at 350 nm of probe and substrate DCM-extract collected at 6 mm HAB in order to get insights on the composition of the DCM-extract as a function of the collection method.

The probe DCM-extract presents a broad emission in the 360–600 nm wavelength region peaked in the 400–460 nm, while substrate DCM-extract presents a redshifted emission spectrum peaked at 470 nm. More evident differences between the two DCM-extracts are observed in the synchronous fluorescence spectra, involving the simultaneous scanning of excitation and emission wavelengths [50,51] using a Δλ = 10 nm. Synchronous fluorescence spectrum of a mixture consisted of the most abundant PAH detected in flame samples is also reported in Fig. 4. Its composition, ranging from two- to seven-ring PAH is reported in a previous paper by Apicella et al. (mix 1 in Ref. 51). Synchronous peaks of individual PAH molecules were clearly distinguishable in the probe DCM-extract, whereas substrate DCM-extract presents peaks only at higher wavelengths mainly due to PAH composed of more than seven rings (C > 24) as also confirmed by the GC–MS analysis of the substrate DCM-extract. Indeed a GC–MS analysis of substrate DCM-extract was unable to see PAH up to 300 u. Thus APPI mass spectrometry involving the direct injection of the DCM-extract has been applied to probe and substrate DCM-extracts as more sensitive technique for high mass PAH analysis. The mass spectra obtained are reported in Fig. 5 together with the mass spectrum of the solvent (toluene) used. Toluene has been used as dopant and as solvent for the sample solutions since it is promptly ionized by the light beam of Kr lamp (the ionization source of APPI mass spectrometer employed) and acts as protonating agent with respect to sample molecules, when they present ionization energy (IE) higher than the photon energy (as in the case of PAH). In order to evaluate the solvent interference, pure toluene has been firstly analyzed by APPI-ion trap MS [31]. The mass spectrum of pure toluene, reported in Fig. 5a, presents the base peak at m/z 91 (tropylium ion) and the odd-electron molecular ion (m/z 92 assigned to the radical cation of toluene C7H8.[52]). Other ion groups are present at higher masses (Fig. 5a); in particular, the ion groups peaked at m/z 108 and at m/z 199 were attributed to reaction products of toluene with oxygen with one and two aromatic rings, respectively [53]. Therefore, toluene interference is easily recognizable on the basis of the peculiar peak sequence structure. In the probe DCM-extract the spectrum (Fig. 5b) is dominated by the PAH sequence, from m/z 128 up to about 600, found for flame-formed PAH [35]. The PAH mass spectrum is peaked around m/z 202 and typically presents two sequences with two gaps, at m/z 12 and 24, attributed in previous works to even- and odd-numbered PAH[35,54,55]. The gap of m/z 24 is attributed to a neat sequential addition of C2 as an ethylene bridge, while a m/z 12 gap is attributed to the insertion of a methylene (–CH2–) into a bay region of angular PAH. The gap at m/z 26, which is sometimes present, especially for the higher MW PAH, indicates the same PAH sequence but with more hydrogenated PAH.

The complete absence of PAH up to m/z 252 is remarkable in the substrate DCM-extract spectrum presenting only toluene peaks in this range. From m/z 252 the typical PAH sequence peaked at around m/z 300–350 is instead noticeable.

Overall, spectroscopy and mass spectrometry have shown that the thermophoretic sampling mode set up in this work is able to catch only condensed phases enriched in heavier aromatic species (C ≥ 22–24). Fig. 6 displays the organic fraction weight percentage of the total carbon particulate matter deposited on the substrate and sampled with the probe. Actually, from the point of view of effective sampling, thermophoretic sampling does not allow the complete recovering of the organic fraction. By contrast, just this selectivity is a useful method to pre-separate, already at the sampling step, soot precursors rendering easier their further analysis. These species have shown interesting optical properties in terms of high and blue-peaked fluorescence emission. Moreover, the flame reactor allows a one-step controllable and tunable synthesis of these species [56,57]. This combination of features makes them attractive as carbon dot candidates [58].

The present work proposes and analyzes thermophoretic deposition as an experimental strategy to allow rapid sampling of nascent soot together with soot precursors deprived of the low-MW compounds (C ≤ 22–24). Soot and soot precursors formed in a heavily-sooting laminar premixed ethylene flame and sampled by a water-cooled probe have been analyzed in terms of optical properties and mass and compared with those thermophoretically sampled on a glass plate in the same flame. Gravimetric analysis along with mass spectrometry, UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy showed that the amount of dichloromethane-extract of carbon particulate matter thermophoretically deposited on the substrate was much lower than that sampled by a probe as it is composed only by high molecular weight condensed phases (C ≥ 22–24). Hence, thermophoretic deposition was demonstrated to selectively sampling soot precursors allowing their further analysis greatly reducing the interference of more abundant small polycyclic aromatic compounds (C ≤ 22–24). Moreover this work clearly demonstrates for the first time that condensation and/or deposition of PAH along with soot particles has to be considered for a reliable evaluation of soot properties especially for thermophoretic sampling. The high molecular weight aromatic species thermophoretically collected presented interesting optical properties in terms of size and emission peaked in the blue region that could be of interest for carbon dots application. Size exclusion chromatography coupled with UV–Visible spectroscopy showed the presence of some aggregates already at the beginning of soot inception to be taken into account as their optical properties strongly affect those of the whole carbon particulate matter.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the financial support from Ministero dello Sviluppo Economico–Accordo di Programma MSE-CNR per la Ricerca del Sistema Elettrico Nazionale.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Jander H, Wagner HG (Eds.). Soot Formation in Combustion, An International Round Table Discussion, Commission on Condensation, Academy of Sciences, Göttingen; 1990.
